semantic_description: A cable crossing where some of the stitches in the cable are purled instead of knit, creating a mixed-texture cable. For example, a 4-stitch cable where the 2 center stitches are purled while the outer stitches are knit. Not the same as a purl cross (which has knit stitches traveling over purl background) — here, the purl stitches are WITHIN the cable itself. Common in complex Aran panels where cables have interior texture.

written_forms: sl2 to CN hold back, k1, p1 from left needle, k1, p1 from CN
work cable crossing with purl stitches in center positions
ambiguity_notes: No standard abbreviation. This is a highly pattern-specific composite. 'C4B-p2 center' describes it but will appear only in the individual pattern legend. Recommend treating as pattern-defined only.

This is never standardized — every pattern defines its own version. The legend/glossary is the only reliable source for the exact stitch sequence. The general concept is: cable cross + some stitches purled.

rs: Slip designated stitches to cable needle, hold in position
Work the remaining stitches, purling where indicated by the pattern legend
Work the held stitches from cable needle, purling where indicated

semantic_description: A cable crossing that includes a decrease within the cable itself, reducing the total stitch count. For example, a cable that crosses 3 over 3 but includes a k2tog within one of the groups, reducing from 6 to 5 stitches. Used when cables need to narrow (e.g., at the top of an Aran panel where cables converge). These are always pattern-specific and defined in the legend.

written_forms: sl3 to CN hold back, k2tog, k1 from left needle, k3 from CN
work cable crossing with a decrease embedded in one group
ambiguity_notes: No standard abbreviation exists for a generic cable-with-decrease. These operations are always pattern-defined with a unique legend entry. Cannot assign a general abbreviation without risk of collision.
No standard abbreviation exists. These are always defined per-pattern in the stitch glossary. The concept is: cable cross + decrease within one group.

rs: Work the cable crossing as specified
Include the decrease (k2tog, ssk, etc.) within one of the cable groups as the legend specifies

semantic_description: A cable crossing that includes an increase, adding stitches within the cable. For example, a cable that crosses 2 over 2 but includes a yarn over or M1 within one group. Used when cables need to widen (e.g., at the base of a branching cable panel). Always pattern-specific.

written_forms: work cable crossing with an increase embedded in one group
ambiguity_notes: Same situation as cable_with_decrease. Always pattern-defined.

rs: Work the cable crossing as specified
Include the increase (yo, m1, etc.) within one cable group per legend

semantic_description: A lace composite in which a yarn over and a decrease are paired together as one conceptual chart operation, preserving overall stitch count while creating an eyelet and a compensating decrease. The exact decrease method and lean, such as k2tog, ssk, or a centered decrease, are legend-defined unless the pattern specifies them separately.

written_forms: work the yarn over and accompanying decrease as one lace unit
work yarn over plus decrease as specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: This is a meta-operation (the pairing of a yo with a decrease for stitch-count balance). Not abbreviated in patterns — appears as two separate chart symbols. No standard single-abbreviation form.
This is not a single universal stitch method. It names the semantic lace unit of eyelet plus balancing decrease, while the exact decrease direction remains pattern-specific.

rs: Work the yarn over that creates the eyelet
Work the corresponding decrease specified by the pattern so the pair preserves stitch count

semantic_description: A combination of ktbl and purl worked as a repeating unit to create twisted ribbing. Each knit column is worked through the back loop, creating a tighter, more defined rib with twisted knit stitches. The pattern unit is typically (k1tbl, p1) or (k2tbl, p2). Not a single operation but a frequently referenced composite pattern that appears in instructions as 'work in twisted rib' or 'twisted rib pattern'.

written_forms: work in twisted rib: *k1 tbl, p1; repeat from *
twisted rib pattern: knit through back loop on all knit stitches

rs: *Knit 1 through back loop, purl 1; repeat from * across
ws: *Knit 1 (the purl columns), purl 1 through back loop (the knit columns); repeat from *

semantic_description: A decorative composite operation that creates a star or daisy-like motif. Typically worked by purling 3 together, then working (yarn over, purl the same 3 together again) without dropping them off, creating a gathered cluster that radiates outward. Different from a bobble — the star stitch creates a flat, radiating pattern rather than a raised bump. Often charted as a single symbol but represents multiple micro-operations.

written_forms: star stitch: p3tog but leave on needle, yo, purl same 3 together again, slide off
make star: (p3tog, yo, p3tog) into same 3 stitches
ambiguity_notes: '* st' is ambiguous in written patterns (asterisk is also a repeat marker). In legend context it's usable; in written instructions it would be confusing. Prefer 'star st'.

rs: Purl 3 together, but do NOT slide stitches off left needle
Yarn over
Purl the same 3 stitches together again
Now slide all 3 original stitches off left needle — 3 new stitches created (the p3tog, yo, p3tog)

semantic_description: Also called the blackberry stitch or bramble stitch. A textured composite where groups of stitches alternate between increasing and decreasing: (k1, p1, k1) into one stitch (increase 1→3), then purl 3 together (decrease 3→1). Worked in offset rows to create a bumpy, berry-like allover texture. Each row alternates between the increase and decrease phases.

written_forms: Row 1: *(k1, p1, k1) into next st, p3tog; rep from *
Row 3: *p3tog, (k1, p1, k1) into next st; rep from * (offset)
ambiguity_notes: 'Bramble', 'blackberry', and 'raspberry' are regional names for the same (or very similar) operation. Some patterns treat them as distinct — blackberry is (k1,p1,k1) in same st + p3tog on next row, bramble is a specific Scottish/Irish variant. Use with care.

Trinity, blackberry, bramble, and raspberry stitch are used interchangeably by different sources, though purists argue they differ in the offset pattern. For practical parsing, treat them as the same family.

rs: Increase phase: (k1, p1, k1) into the next stitch — 3 stitches from 1
Decrease phase: p3tog — 1 stitch from 3
Alternate across the row, offset every other repeat row

semantic_description: A gathered or smocked stitch family in which live stitches are drawn together into a decorative pinch or bundle. The gathering may be created by wrapping slipped stitches, by working a gather front and back, or by another pattern-specific gathering method. This is broader than a specific Estonian gathered stitch and distinct from a cluster stitch formed by grouped partial stitches joined together at the top.

written_forms: slip designated stitches, wrap yarn around them, return them to the needle, and work as established
gather designated stitches together as specified in the pattern
ambiguity_notes: Gathered stitches form a broad family. Some are wrapped or smocked gathers, while others are counted regional variants such as Estonian gathers.

rs: Work the designated stitches using the gather method given in the pattern or legend
Keep the stitches structurally live unless the pattern explicitly converts the gather into a decrease

semantic_description: A cluster stitch family where multiple stitches or partial stitches are grouped into one clustered unit. Depending on the pattern, a cluster may be built from several stitches worked into one place and closed together, or from another grouped texture recipe defined in the legend. It is related to gathered stitches but is not the same as a wrapped or smocked gather.

written_forms: work the cluster stitch as specified in the pattern legend
form a clustered unit from the designated stitches
ambiguity_notes: Cluster stitch recipes vary by designer and symbol system. Multi-cell cluster glyphs are often just display-width variants of the same semantic operation, not distinct stitch types.

rs: Work the cluster using the pattern's specified grouped-stitch method
Treat wider chart symbols as visual variants unless the legend defines separate stitch behavior

semantic_description: A counted Estonian or Haapsalu-style gathered stitch used in Baltic lace knitting. The stitches are gathered together and then re-expanded from the gathered point to create a small textured button or gathered star. This family is more specific than a generic gathered or smocked stitch and distinct from an Estonian nupp.

written_forms: work an Estonian gather as specified, often by gathering stitches together and re-knitting from the gathered stitch
ambiguity_notes: Estonian gathered stitches have no standardized abbreviation in any major English-language publisher. 'EGS' is a reasonable app-internal shorthand. Individual pattern designers invent their own. Recommend treating pattern-defined.

rs: Gather the designated stitches together using the pattern's Estonian gather recipe
Re-expand the gathered stitch into the counted form required by the pattern

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 2. In chart systems such as StitchMastery, this marks the two-stitch form of the Estonian gather family and should be kept distinct from the 3-, 4-, or 5-stitch forms for semantic lookup even when the legend is still needed for the exact recipe.

ambiguity_notes: No standard cross-publisher abbreviation. The number suffix (2) indicates stitch count. Knitr-internal convention 'EGS-N' is reasonable.

rs: Work the counted 2-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 3. It represents the three-stitch form of the Estonian gather family and should remain distinct from the other counted forms during semantic lookup.

ambiguity_notes: Same as estonian_gathered_2 — pattern-defined in all published sources.

rs: Work the counted 3-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 4. It represents the four-stitch form of the Estonian gather family and should remain distinct from the other counted forms during semantic lookup.

ambiguity_notes: Pattern-defined. No standard abbreviation.

rs: Work the counted 4-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 5. It represents the five-stitch form of the Estonian gather family and should remain distinct from the other counted forms during semantic lookup.

ambiguity_notes: Pattern-defined. No standard abbreviation.

rs: Work the counted 5-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

rs: Work the counted 6-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

rs: Work the counted 11-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 12. It represents the twelve-stitch form of the Estonian gather family and should remain distinct from the other counted forms during semantic lookup.

ambiguity_notes: Pattern-defined. No standard abbreviation.

rs: Work the counted 12-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 13. Consumes 13 live stitches and gathers them into a single narrow cluster on the next row. Used in Estonian lace borders and Haapsalu shawls.

ambiguity_notes: Pattern-defined. No CYC standard.

rs: Work the counted 13-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 14. Consumes 14 live stitches and gathers them into a single narrow cluster on the next row. Used in Estonian lace borders and Haapsalu shawls.

ambiguity_notes: Pattern-defined. No CYC standard.

rs: Work the counted 14-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 15. Consumes 15 live stitches and gathers them into a single narrow cluster on the next row. Used in Estonian lace borders and Haapsalu shawls.

ambiguity_notes: Pattern-defined. No CYC standard.

rs: Work the counted 15-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A counted Estonian gathered stitch variant labeled 16. Consumes 16 live stitches and gathers them into a single narrow cluster on the next row. Used in Estonian lace borders and Haapsalu shawls.

ambiguity_notes: Pattern-defined. No CYC standard.

rs: Work the counted 16-stitch form of the Estonian gathered stitch as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: Decorated Decrease (Unspecified) — a chart annotation where a base decrease is overlaid with a decorator (number, bead marker, or other glyph) that carries pattern-specific meaning defined in the legend. The knitting action is the underlying decrease; the overlay conveys extra information (e.g., cross-reference number, bead placement, style indicator). Appears as a single symbol in Stitchmastery charts where numbered decreases/increases are used to disambiguate repeats or match written instructions.

written_forms: work a decrease as specified in the legend; the overlay number or marker is a chart cross-reference
ambiguity_notes: No CYC standard. The overlay (number 1-9, bead, or style marker) is a chart annotation, not a distinct knitting action. The actual manipulation is always a decrease whose specific form (k2tog, ssk, cdd, etc.) is defined in the pattern legend per-decoration.

rs: Identify the underlying decrease from the pattern legend (the overlay number or marker references a specific stitch)
Work that decrease as specified

The knitting action is the underlying yarn over; the overlay conveys extra information (e.g., cross-reference number, bead placement, style indicator).
